title: The Rose.
content:
آ آ آ آ The rose had been washï؟½d, just washï؟½d in a shower,
آ آ آ آ Which Mary to Anna conveyï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ The plentiful moisture encumberï؟½d the flower,
آ آ آ آ And weighï؟½d down its beautiful head.


آ آ آ آ The cup was all fillï؟½d, and the leaves were all wet,
آ آ آ آ And it seemï؟½d, to a fanciful view,
آ آ آ آ To weep for the buds it had left, with regret,
آ آ آ آ On the flourishing bush where it grew.


آ آ آ آ I hastily seized it, unfit as it was
آ آ آ آ For a nosegay, so dripping and drownï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And swinging it rudely, too rudely, alas!
آ آ آ آ I snappï؟½d it, it fell to the ground.


آ آ آ آ And such, I exclaimï؟½d, is the pitiless part
آ آ آ آ Some act by the delicate mind,
آ آ آ آ Regardless of wringing and breaking a heart
آ آ آ آ Already to sorrow resignï؟½d.


آ آ آ آ This elegant rose, had I shaken it less,
آ آ آ آ Might have bloomï؟½d with its owner a while;
آ آ آ آ And the tear, that is wiped with a little address,
آ آ آ آ May be followï؟½d perhaps by a smile.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Cowper represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of William Cowper's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
